audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel / Shemuel Aleph chapter 30, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Ziklag's burning, captives, collective grief, threatened stoning, ephod inquiry, Besor exhaustion, abandoned Egyptian servant, food and restored strength, negotiated safety, prolonged attack, recovery, spoil terminology, equal-share ruling, and political gift distribution.

verses.restored: When David and his men came to Ziklag on third day, Amalekites had made a raid against Negev and against Ziklag. They struck Ziklag and burned it with fire.
They took captive women who were in it, from small to great. They did not put anyone to death, but led them away and went on their way.
David and his men came to city, and look, it was burned with fire, and their wives and their sons and their daughters had been taken captive.
David and people who were with him lifted their voice and wept until there was no strength in them to weep.
David's two wives were taken captive: Ahinoam the Jezreelite and Abigail wife of Nabal the Carmelite.
David was greatly distressed, because people said to stone him, because soul of all people was bitter, each over his sons and over his daughters. But David strengthened himself in YHWH his Divine.
David said to Abiathar the priest, son of Ahimelech, Please bring ephod near to me. Abiathar brought ephod near to David.
David inquired of YHWH, saying, Shall I pursue after this band? Shall I overtake it? YHWH said to him, Pursue, because overtaking you shall overtake, and rescuing you shall rescue.
David went, he and six hundred men who were with him, and they came to wadi Besor, where those left behind remained.
David pursued, he and four hundred men, and two hundred men remained, who were too exhausted to cross wadi Besor.
They found an Egyptian man in field and took him to David. They gave him bread and he ate, and they gave him water to drink.
They gave him a slice of cake of figs and two clusters/cakes of raisins. He ate, and his spirit returned to him, because he had not eaten bread and had not drunk water three days and three nights.
David said to him, To whom do you belong, and from where are you? He said, I am an Egyptian young man, servant of an Amalekite man. My master abandoned me because I became sick three days ago.
We raided Negev of Cherethites, what belongs to Judah, and Negev of Caleb, and Ziklag we burned with fire.

David said to him, Will you bring me down to this band? He said, Swear to me by Divine that you will not put me to death and will not shut me into hand of my master, and I will bring you down to this band.
He brought him down, and look, they were spread upon face of all land, eating and drinking and celebrating over all great spoil they had taken from land of Philistines and from land of Judah.
David struck them from twilight until evening of next day. Not a man of them escaped except four hundred young men who rode upon camels and fled.
David rescued all that Amalekites had taken, and David rescued his two wives.
Nothing of theirs was missing, from small even to great, even sons and daughters, and from spoil even to all that they had taken for themselves. David returned all.
David took all flock and herd. They drove them before that livestock and said, This is David's spoil.

David came to two hundred men who had been too exhausted to walk after David, whom they had made remain at wadi Besor. They went out to meet David and to meet people who were with him. David approached people and asked concerning their peace.

Every evil and worthless man among men who had gone with David answered and said, Because they did not go with us, we will not give them from spoil that we rescued, except each man's wife and children, and let them lead them away and go.
David said, You shall not do so, my brothers, with what YHWH has given us. YHWH guarded us and gave into our hand band that came against us.
Who will listen to you concerning this matter? For as portion of one who goes down into battle, so portion of one who stays by baggage; together they shall divide.
From that day and onward, he set it as statute and judgment for Israel until this day.
David came to Ziklag and sent from spoil to elders of Judah, to his companions, saying, Look, a blessing/gift for you from spoil of enemies of YHWH.
To those in Bethel, to those in Ramoth of Negev, and to those in Jattir,
and to those in Aroer, to those in Siphmoth, and to those in Eshtemoa,
and to those in Racal, to those in cities of Jerahmeelites, and to those in cities of Kenites,
and to those in Hormah, to those in Bor-ashan, and to those in Athach,
and to those in Hebron, and to all places where David had walked about, he and his men.

translation_notes: The women and children are captives and survivors, not property or 'spoil.' Recovery does not erase trauma or establish that all abductees were unharmed.
Collective weeping is presented without shame. Grief, exhaustion, anger, and loss of strength are embodied human responses.
The threat to stone David shows how traumatized communities can redirect rage inward. Distress explains but does not justify mob killing.

The Egyptian servant is abandoned when ill, deprived of food and water, and vulnerable to both armies. David's group feeds him before interrogation, and he negotiates explicit safety conditions before assisting.
His designation as Egyptian and servant/slave must not support xenophobia or slavery apologetics. He is a crucial agent with intelligence, memory, and bargaining power.
The battle remains lethal and prolonged. Rescue of captives does not make every act of violence, spoil-taking, or later political distribution ethically clean.
The two hundred are too exhausted to continue. David's equal-share rule rejects ableism, productivity hierarchy, and contempt for logistical or caregiving labor.
Returning wives and children is not a sufficient 'share'; people are not compensation. The disputed material goods are divided equally.
David's gifts to Judah also build political relationships and should not be spiritualized away from patronage and succession strategy.
safety_note: Center the captives, the abandoned Egyptian worker, the exhausted two hundred, and the grieving community. Do not treat people as spoil, shame trauma or fatigue, justify slavery, or glorify the retaliatory battle. The chapter's clearest constructive ethic is food before extraction, negotiated safety, full recovery, and equal sharing across different forms of labor.
